questions.question: A traction splint is indicated for:
questions.rationale: Traction splints immobilize mid-shaft femur fractures only.

questions.question: Traction splints should NOT be used with:
options: A suspected pelvic, hip, knee, or lower-leg injury
questions.rationale: Hip, knee, lower-leg, ankle, and pelvic injuries contraindicate traction splinting.

questions.question: Applying traction across a fractured/dislocated knee risks:
questions.rationale: Traction at the knee can lacerate the popliteal vessels behind the joint.

questions.question: Benefits of a femur traction splint include all EXCEPT:
questions.rationale: Traction splints realign femur fractures and reduce pain/blood loss but are not for hip dislocations.

questions.question: Which is a low-profile splint that applies traction from a single bar between the legs?
questions.rationale: The Sager splint uses a single medial bar, is lower-profile, and can splint both legs.

questions.question: Before and after applying a traction splint, assess:
questions.rationale: Distal PMS is documented before and after splinting to detect neurovascular compromise.

questions.question: How much traction should be applied with a traction splint?
options: Only enough to realign and relieve pain
questions.rationale: Apply only enough traction to realign and reduce pain; excessive traction harms neurovascular structures.

questions.question: A pelvic binder is correctly centered over the:
questions.rationale: Binding at the greater trochanters closes the pelvic ring to control hemorrhage.

questions.question: A suspected unstable pelvis should be assessed:
questions.rationale: Assess once, gently; repeated manipulation can worsen hemorrhage.

questions.question: A tourniquet should be placed:
options: 2–3 inches proximal to the wound, not over a joint
questions.rationale: Place the tourniquet 2–3 inches proximal to the wound, avoiding joints.

questions.question: A correctly applied tourniquet should be tightened until:
options: Bleeding stops and the distal pulse is eliminated
questions.rationale: Tighten until bleeding stops and the distal pulse disappears; a partial (venous) tourniquet increases bleeding.

questions.question: When applying a tourniquet, you must always:
questions.rationale: Document the application time; periodic loosening is not done and the device should remain visible.

questions.question: Periodically loosening a properly applied tourniquet is:
options: Harmful and should not be done
questions.rationale: Loosening a tourniquet can worsen hemorrhage and is not recommended in the field.

questions.question: Hemostatic agents are best used for:
options: Junctional/deep wounds where a tourniquet cannot be applied
questions.rationale: Hemostatic dressings are packed into junctional or deep wounds and held with sustained pressure.

questions.question: A complication of excessive traction with a traction splint is:
questions.rationale: Too much traction can damage nerves and vessels—apply only enough to realign and relieve pain.

questions.question: Why is a traction splint deferred for a critical load-and-go patient?
options: It is time-consuming and delays transport
questions.rationale: Traction splinting takes time; for load-and-go patients, package and transport, applying it en route if at all.

questions.question: A pelvic binder placed too high over the iliac crests will:
options: Fail to effectively close the pelvic ring
questions.rationale: Only binding at the trochanters effectively compresses the pelvic ring; placement over the crests is ineffective.

questions.question: Which device minimizes movement when transferring a pelvic-fracture patient?
options: Long backboard with a log roll
questions.rationale: A scoop stretcher slides under the patient from both sides, minimizing pelvic manipulation.
